title: Compounding of Contraventions
content:
Session 3: Compounding of Offences 
Dinesh Garg [2019 | RBI] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã The applicant, Dineshkumar Garg, is an Indian resident ‚Üí transferred 378 shares of an Indian company to a non-resident 
‚Üí in return, foreign exchange flowed into India through banking channels. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Nature of Allegation 
179 
 
‚û¢‚Äã RBI alleged contravention of FEMA provisions, specifically in relation to pricing/valuation of shares + notification 
of the transaction. 
‚û¢‚Äã Notification u/ Regulation 10 A (b) (i) read with paragraph 10 of Schedule I to Notification No. FEMA 20 ‚Üí 
form FCTRS has to be submitted to the Authorised Dealer Bank within 60 days from the date of receipt or 
payment of the amount of consideration ‚Üí onus is on the transferor/transferee Resident in India. 
‚û¢‚Äã Valuation u/ Regulation 10A(b)(i) of FEMA 20 read with Para 5 of Schedule 1 to FEMA 20 ‚Üí the price of such 
shares shall not be less than the valuation of shares done as per any internationally accepted pricing methodology 
for valuation of shares on arm‚Äôs length basis, duly certified by a Chartered Accountant or a SEBI registered 
Merchant Banker where the shares of the company are not listed on any recognised stock exchange in India ‚Üí the 
shares were undervalued at the time of transfer. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Shares were priced too low. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Proper valuation would have resulted in higher forex inflow. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
The pricing shortfall was approximately ‚Çπ8 lakhs. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Proceedings 
‚û¢‚Äã The applicant sought compounding of the alleged contravention. 
‚û¢‚Äã RBI considered the matter under its compounding powers. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 21, NDI Rules 
‚û¢‚Äã Share valuation for transfer between residents and non-residents must be conducted in accordance with prescribed 
valuation norms. 
‚û¢‚Äã Intended to ensure fair pricing and prevent loss of foreign exchange to India. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 13(1), FEMA 
‚û¢‚Äã For a quantifiable contravention: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Penalty may extend to three times the sum involved. 
‚û¢‚Äã In this case: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Quantifiable amount ‚âà ‚Çπ8 lakhs. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Maximum possible penalty ‚âà ‚Çπ24 lakhs. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Compounding under FEMA 
‚û¢‚Äã Allows settlement of contraventions without prolonged adjudication. 
‚û¢‚Äã Authorities may consider: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Nature of transaction. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Intent. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Manner of execution (banking vs illegal channels). 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Legality of the Transaction 
‚û¢‚Äã The share transfer itself was not prohibited. 
‚û¢‚Äã Forex inflow is generally consistent with FEMA‚Äôs objectives. 
‚û¢‚Äã Thus, this was not a case of capital flight or illicit remittance. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Core Problem: Valuation 
‚û¢‚Äã RBI‚Äôs dissatisfaction stemmed from undervaluation of shares. 
‚û¢‚Äã Undervaluation meant: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
India received less foreign exchange than it should have. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
This indirectly undermines foreign exchange management policy. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Why RBI Still Took a Lenient View 
‚û¢‚Äã The transaction was legitimate. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Took place through authorised banking channels. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Was not a hawala or clandestine transaction. 
‚û¢‚Äã There was no evidence of: 
180 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Mala fide intent. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Money laundering or evasion. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Penalty Determination 
‚û¢‚Äã Although RBI had statutory power to impose up to ‚Çπ24 lakhs, imposed only ‚Çπ76,000. 
 
Binani Industries Limited [2019 | RBI] 
Facts:  
‚ùñ‚Äã The applicant filed an application dated 16 December 2015 seeking compounding of contraventions under FEMA, and 
‚ùñ‚Äã Binani incorporated an SPV outside India in Luxembourg. This SPV was constituted for acquiring 100% stake in some 
other company. It also held 100% share in GGFL, in Goa. They merged this GGFL (a resident) company with the SPV (a 
non-resident company). 
‚ùñ‚Äã The creation of a step-down subsidiary in India through an overseas entity was not permitted under Regulation 6(2)(ii) of 
FEM (Transfer or Issue of Any Foreign Security) Regulations, 2004 ‚Üí not considered a bona fide business activity of the 
overseas entity under FEMA. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Despite the violation RBI granted post-facto approval to the acquisition ‚Üí it was aimed at business synergy and 
consolidation, not regulatory evasion. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The applicant admitted the contraventions + claimed the lapse was unintentional + requested a lenient view. 
 
Issues:  
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the applicant contravened Regulation 6(2)(ii) of Notification by setting up a step-down subsidiary in India 
through an overseas SPV. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the receipt of foreign direct investment under an ODI structure qualifies as a bona fide business activity under the 
Automatic Route. 
‚ùñ‚Äã What should be the quantum of penalty under Section 13 of FEMA, considering the amount involved, the duration of 
contravention, and the applicant‚Äôs conduct. 
 
Rule: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Regulation 6(2)(ii), FEMA 120/2004-RB 
‚û¢‚Äã Permits an Indian party to make direct investment in an overseas JV or WOS only if the overseas entity is engaged 
in a bona fide business activity. 
‚û¢‚Äã Receiving FDI in India under an ODI structure is not a bona fide business activity permissible under the 
Automatic Route. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Section 13, FEMA, 1999 ‚Üí Provides that any person contravening FEMA is liable to a penalty up to three times the sum 
involved. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Foreign Exchange (Compounding Proceedings) Rules, 2000 
‚û¢‚Äã Enable RBI to compound admitted contraventions. 
‚û¢‚Äã Rule 10 applies in case of failure to pay the compounded amount within the stipulated time. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Existence of Contravention 
‚û¢‚Äã The applicant‚Äôs overseas SPV (3B Binani) acquired GGFL, resulting in a step-down subsidiary in India. 
‚û¢‚Äã Such structuring violated Regulation 6(2)(ii) as the overseas entity was effectively receiving FDI under an ODI 
transaction. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The applicant admitted the contravention during the personal hearing ‚Üí claimed: 
‚û¢‚Äã The violation was procedural and unintentional. 
‚û¢‚Äã Amount involved: ‚Çπ155,92,33,934 ‚Üí representing inward remittances received from 3B Binani on various dates. 
‚û¢‚Äã Period of contravention approximately four years. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Discretion in Penalty 
181 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Although Section 13 permits penalty up to three times the amount involved, RBI exercised discretionary leniency, 
considering: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Business purpose of the transaction, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Post-facto approval already granted, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Absence of mala fide intent, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Admission of guilt by the applicant. 
 
Times Internet Ltd. v. Directorate of Enforcement [2024, DHC] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã TIL, a subsidiary of BCCL, and BCCL itself, both persons resident in India under FEMA, operate digital media and 
internet-based businesses through wholly owned subsidiaries abroad.  
‚û¢‚Äã As part of their regular business strategy, TIL invested approximately USD 30.9 million between 2006 and 2015 in 
its US subsidiary under the automatic route, while BCCL incorporated BCCL Worldwide Inc. in 2015, with both 
petitioners asserting full compliance with FEMA reporting requirements.  
‚ùñ‚Äã In 2021, the ED issued summons under s.37(1) of FEMA seeking information on these overseas direct investments, which 
the petitioners duly furnished, though no adjudication or enforcement proceedings followed.  
‚ùñ‚Äã After the notification of the liberalised FEMA (Overseas Investment) Rules, 2022, the petitioners sought to make further 
overseas investments in 2023 and were required under Rule 10 to obtain a NOC from the ED on the ground that they were 
allegedly under investigation.  
‚ùñ‚Äã The ED rejected the NOC applications in October‚ÄìNovember 2023 without assigning reasons and shortly thereafter issued 
fresh summons, nearly two years after the earlier ones, prompting the petitioners to approach the DHC challenging the 
rejection of the NOC and the arbitrary exercise of ED‚Äôs powers. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the issuance of summons under Section 37(1) of FEMA amounts to an ‚Äúinvestigation‚Äù justifying denial of NOC 
under Rule 10 of the FEMA (OI) Rules, 2022. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether ED was justified in rejecting the NOC based on allegations of overvaluation in earlier ODI transactions. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether denial of NOC, without any concluded finding of contravention under FEMA, is arbitrary and violative of 
principles of natural justice. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether the petitioners‚Äô overseas investments qualify as ‚Äúbona fide business activity‚Äù under Rule 9 of the FEMA (OI) Rules, 
2022. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA, 1999 
‚û¢‚Äã s.37(1) ‚Äì empowers ED to issue summons for production of documents and information + does not, by itself, 
establish guilt or contravention. 
‚û¢‚Äã S.4 ‚Äì prohibits certain capital account transactions except as permitted under rules and regulations. 
‚û¢‚Äã s.13 ‚Äì prescribes fiscal penalties for FEMA violations: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Up to three times the amount involved, or 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
‚Çπ2 lakhs where the amount is not quantifiable. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA (Overseas Investment) Rules, 2022 ‚Äì notified under Section 46 of FEMA to liberalise and streamline overseas 
investments. 
‚û¢‚Äã Rule 9 ‚Äì Overseas Investment ‚Üí permits overseas investment for ‚Äúbona fide business activity‚Äù: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Directly, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Through a step-down subsidiary, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Or through a Special Purpose Vehicle (SPV). 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Explanation defines ‚Äúbona fide business activity‚Äù as one: 
‚óè‚Äã
Permissible under Indian law, and 
‚óè‚Äã
Permissible under the law of the host country. 
182 
 
‚û¢‚Äã Rule 10 ‚Äì No Objection Certificate (NOC) ‚Äì applicable when the person is under investigation by ED, CBI, SFIO, 
etc ‚Üí requires the investigating agency to issue NOC or record objections within 60 days. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Proviso introduces a deemed no-objection if no response is received within 60 days. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
NOC is addressed to the Authorised Dealer (AD Bank). 
‚ùñ‚Äã FEMA OI Directions, 2022 
‚û¢‚Äã Place responsibility on Authorised Dealers to ensure FEMA compliance. 
‚û¢‚Äã ADs are liable under Sections 11 and 13 of FEMA for facilitating illegal remittances. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã ED‚Äôs Justification for NOC Rejection 
‚û¢‚Äã ED alleged that during ODI transactions in 2017‚Äì2018, TIL invested in MX Media Co. through overvaluation of 
shares ‚Üí according to ED: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Inflated valuations created a ‚Äúmaze‚Äù facilitating excess outflow of foreign exchange. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Valuations relied on figures recorded in Share Subscription and Shareholders‚Äô Agreements. 
‚û¢‚Äã Court‚Äôs Evaluation of ED‚Äôs Conduct 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Investigations had allegedly been ongoing since 2017 + no adjudication or finding of contravention. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Continuous summons without closure = unreasonable. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Mere suspicion or inquiry does not amount to an investigation warranting denial of NOC. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Valuation Argument 
‚û¢‚Äã The Court noted that FEMA mandates valuation by: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
SEBI-registered Category I Merchant Bankers, or 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Overseas merchant bankers accredited by host regulators. 
‚û¢‚Äã Petitioners had complied with this requirement. 
‚û¢‚Äã ED‚Äôs allegation of overvaluation was unsupported by formal proceedings. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Expert valuation relied upon by petitioners had probative value unless displaced through adjudication. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Natural Justice and Proportionality 
‚û¢‚Äã Denial of NOC had the effect of completely restraining petitioners from investing abroad. 
‚û¢‚Äã FEMA violations attract fiscal penalties, not blanket business prohibitions. 
‚û¢‚Äã Such drastic consequences cannot flow from mere issuance of summons, without findings under s.4 of FEMA. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Rule 10 and Deemed Approval 
‚û¢‚Äã Rule 10 reflects a liberalised regime ‚Üí failure to issue NOC within stipulated timelines points toward deemed 
no-objection ‚Üí HERE, ED failed to provide clear, cogent, and rational reasons for rejection. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Role of Authorised Dealer 
‚û¢‚Äã It is the Authorised Dealer Bank, not ED, that primarily ensures FEMA compliance ‚Üí regulatory safeguards 
remain intact even after NOC issuance. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Nexus Requirement 
‚û¢‚Äã HERE, ED failed to establish any nexus between the alleged past contraventions and the proposed fresh overseas 
investment. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Subjecting compliant entities to indefinite uncertainty was held to be unreasonable. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã DHC quashed the impugned rejection letters and held that: 
‚û¢‚Äã Mere issuance of summons under s.37(1) of FEMA does not justify denial of NOC. 
‚û¢‚Äã Absence of findings under s.4 of FEMA or Income Tax Act provisions is fatal to ED‚Äôs case. 
‚û¢‚Äã ED failed to demonstrate any concluded FEMA contravention. 
‚ùñ‚Äã TIL and BCCL are free to approach their Authorised Dealer for remittance. 
‚ùñ‚Äã The proposed remittance shall be processed on its own merits, under applicable FEMA rules. 
 
183 
 
Noy Vallesina Engineering Spa vs. Jindal Drugs Limited [2006 | BHC | 1J] 
Facts: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Noy Vallesina Engineering SpA, an Italian company, became a party to a contract with Jindal Drugs Limited for setting up 
an ascorbic acid plant in India after Enco Engineering Chur AG of Switzerland, the original contracting party, assigned the 
agreements to it in 1995 with Jindal‚Äôs consent.  
‚ùñ‚Äã Disputes arose and were referred to ICC arbitration seated in London, resulting in a partial award in 2000 and a final award 
in 2001 in favour of Noy Vallesina.  
‚ùñ‚Äã When enforcement of the foreign award was sought in India, Jindal opposed it under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 
1996, arguing that the underlying engineering and technology agreements lacked RBI approval, that payment would violate 
Section 9 of FERA, 1973, and that enforcement of the award was therefore contrary to Indian foreign exchange law and 
public policy. 
 
Issues: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether absence of RBI approval under foreign exchange laws renders the arbitral award unenforceable as being against 
public policy of India. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether FERA/FEMA can be used as a defence or shield to resist performance of contractual obligations or enforcement of 
an arbitral award. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Whether RBI permission under Section 9 of FERA, 1973 is: 
‚û¢‚Äã A condition precedent to entering into contracts, or 
‚û¢‚Äã Only required at the stage of actual payment. 
 
Rules: 
‚ùñ‚Äã s9, FERA 1973 
‚û¢‚Äã Requires RBI permission for certain foreign exchange payments. 
‚û¢‚Äã Does not prohibit entering into contracts per se. 
‚ùñ‚Äã s47(3), FERA 1973 explicitly provides that: 
‚û¢‚Äã Legal proceedings may be brought in India to recover sums due as debt or damages. 
‚û¢‚Äã However, execution or enforcement of payment requires RBI/Central Government permission. 
‚û¢‚Äã Crucially states that foreign exchange law shall not prevent legal proceedings for recovery. 
‚ùñ‚Äã s21, FERA 1947 
‚û¢‚Äã Earlier provision with similar wording. 
‚û¢‚Äã Explicitly clarified that foreign exchange law cannot be used as a shield to avoid contractual liability. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Public Policy under Section 48, Arbitration Act, 1996 ‚Üí enforcement of a foreign award may be refused if contrary to: 
‚û¢‚Äã Fundamental policy of Indian law, 
‚û¢‚Äã Interests of India, 
‚û¢‚Äã Justice or morality. 
‚û¢‚Äã Narrow interpretation as laid down in Renusagar Power Co. v. General Electric Co. 
 
Application: 
‚ùñ‚Äã On RBI Permission and Section 9, FERA 
‚û¢‚Äã Accepted the petitioner‚Äôs submission that s9 requires permission only when payment is made, not when the 
contract is entered into ‚Üí not necessarily prior; it can be obtained ex post facto. 
‚û¢‚Äã Therefore, absence of RBI approval at the time of contracting does not invalidate the agreement or the award. 
‚ùñ‚Äã FERA Cannot Be Used as a Shield 
‚û¢‚Äã Relying heavily on Renusagar ‚Üí s.47(3) FERA is precisely to prevent parties from evading contractual obligations 
by invoking foreign exchange laws [which regulates how payment is made, not whether liability exists] ‚Üí allowing 
such a defence would enable Indian parties to: 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Enter into contracts freely, 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Enjoy benefits, 
184 
 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
And later avoid payment by pleading illegality. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Public Policy Argument Rejected 
‚û¢‚Äã PP under Section 48 is narrow (Renusagar standard) ‚Üí no statutory provision prohibiting an arbitral tribunal 
from awarding money to a foreign entity under an unapproved contract. 
‚ñÝ‚Äã
Enforcement may be regulated, but liability is unaffected. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Transfer of Responsibility to Decree Holder 
‚û¢‚Äã s47(3) implicitly transfers the burden of obtaining RBI permission from the judgment debtor to the decree-holder 
at the enforcement stage ‚Üí thus, enforcement is subject to RBI permission, but the award itself remains valid. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Policy and Commercial Considerations 
‚û¢‚Äã A contrary interpretation would make India appear hostile to international commerce and arbitration ‚Üí would 
undermine confidence in enforcement of foreign awards. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ùñ‚Äã Absence of RBI approval does not make the arbitral award unenforceable. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Foreign exchange laws cannot be invoked as a defence to resist enforcement of contractual or arbitral liabilities. 
‚ùñ‚Äã RBI permission, if required, can be obtained ex post facto at the stage of payment. 
‚ùñ‚Äã Final Position 
‚û¢‚Äã The award is not contrary to public policy of India. 
‚û¢‚Äã FEMA/FERA regulate execution of payment, not existence of liability. 
‚û¢‚Äã Enforcement cannot be defeated by invoking foreign exchange laws as a shield.
